title: The Power of ‘Godzilla Minus One’ and an Awards Season Mega-Mailbag
format: Godzilla Minus One sneaks up on everybody and turns into the weekend's real event movie, then a mailbag stretches across AFI's list, the Best Picture belt, and a scene-by-scene tour of the year.

films.title: Godzilla Minus One
films.note: Toho's first in-house Godzilla movie in eight years turns into a word-of-mouth sensation, a $15 million post-WWII war drama with better effects than movies costing twenty times as much. Both hosts call it one of the year's most fun theater experiences, even with a real emotional undertow.

films.title: Godzilla vs. Kong
films.note: The last Godzilla movie they covered on the show, back at the height of the pandemic, brought up as a tonal contrast to how sincere Minus One plays it.

films.title: Godzilla
films.note: The Gareth Edwards version, cited as the closest tonal cousin: played straight as tragedy rather than spectacle.

films.title: Shin Godzilla
films.note: The other modern Toho Godzilla Sean says he liked going in.

films.title: Oppenheimer
films.note: An unofficial pairing: both films tell the nuclear story from the side usually left out of American movies.

films.title: I Live in Fear
films.note: Kurosawa's nuclear-anxiety drama, cited as one of the films Minus One's subtext draws on.

films.title: Grave of the Fireflies
films.note: The Ghibli film named as another entry in the post-war-Japan-and-nuclear-anxiety canon.

segments.heading: A Big Year for Japanese Film

films.title: The Boy and the Heron
films.note: Teased for next week's show; part of Bob's top five.

films.title: Godzilla x Kong: The New Empire
films.note: The new trailer, dropped just days earlier, prompted a quick aside about Legendary's side of the Godzilla rights.

films.title: Evil Does Not Exist
films.note: Not yet released in the U.S.; missed out on Japan's Oscar submission slot.

films.title: Monster
films.note: Hirokazu Kore-eda's latest, released the same week.

films.title: Perfect Days
films.note: Wim Wenders' Koji Yakusho vehicle; Sean has a screener to loan Amanda.

films.title: Fallen Leaves
films.note: Aki Kaurismäki's latest, which Sean liked a lot.

segments.heading: Poor Things (An Early Look)
segments.note: A full conversation is planned for January once the film is in wide release

films.title: Poor Things
films.note: Yorgos Lanthimos' feminist Frankenstein fable with Emma Stone, hotly touted out of Venice and Telluride, now cooling off during a slow platform release.

films.title: The Favourite
films.note: Lanthimos and screenwriter Tony McNamara's previous collaboration.

films.title: Eileen
films.note: William Oldroyd's Ottessa Moshfegh adaptation with Thomasin McKenzie and Anne Hathaway; Sean liked it without loving it, praising Hathaway's swing away from glamour in the back half.

films.title: Carol
films.note: The film Eileen's opening stretch initially evokes before it turns into something else.

films.title: Last Night in Soho
films.note: Cited for McKenzie's similar innocent-ingenue register.

films.title: Leave No Trace
films.note: Another McKenzie performance Sean singles out.

films.title: Lady Macbeth
films.note: Oldroyd's previous film, which Sean says he liked even more than Eileen.

films.title: Oppenheimer
films.note: Amanda guesses it correctly on the AFI Top 10; also the star-driven Best Picture front-runner most of this mailbag keeps circling back to.

films.title: Killers of the Flower Moon
films.note: Subject of a screen-time trivia round (De Niro clocks in at 102 minutes, Lily Gladstone at 56) that Sean argues is the wrong way to measure Gladstone's impact.

films.title: May December
films.note: Charles Melton's Independent Spirit nomination and Gotham win get discussed as an early sign of momentum against Robert Downey Jr.'s Oppenheimer campaign; also its own screen-time trivia round.

films.title: All of Us Strangers
films.note: Guessed, incorrectly, as part of the AFI list.

films.title: The Color Purple
films.note: Left off both AFI and NBR's lists, which the hosts call a blow given its Golden Globes musical/comedy chances.

films.title: Dark Waters
films.note: Cited in a detour on Todd Haynes' recent run as a for-hire director rather than a writer-director.

films.title: The Iron Claw
films.note: Still embargoed; Sean hasn't seen it yet.

films.title: Memory
films.note: Michel Franco's Jessica Chastain/Peter Sarsgaard drama, which Sean expects to be divisive.

films.title: Beau Is Afraid
films.note: John Waters' number one film of the year, per his year-end list.

films.title: BlackBerry
films.note: Glenn Howerton's Independent Spirit nomination gets a shout-out.

films.title: Parasite
films.note: The unanimous answer in the ‘Best Picture belt’ game: the most recent winner both hosts think still holds up.

films.title: Moonlight
films.note: Named as the film Parasite took the belt from.

films.title: No Country for Old Men
films.note: Traced back through the belt game as the one Spotlight took it from.

films.title: Subject
films.note: The documentary Sean watched about people who appeared in other documentaries, used to explain why it's been a weak year for the format.

films.title: All the Beauty and the Bloodshed
films.note: Named as last year's documentary standout, with no equivalent this year.

films.title: Renaissance: A Film by Beyoncé
films.note: Sean's likely favorite documentary of the year.

films.title: Still: A Michael J. Fox Movie
Bye Bye Barry
films.note: The Barry Sanders documentary named in the same star-driven-docs list.

films.title: The Taste of Things
films.note: Discussed as a possible Juliette Binoche awards vehicle, and later cited for its opening scene.

films.title: Bottoms
films.note: Rachel Sennott named as a Best Actress long shot; Sean calls it the funniest thing he saw all year.

films.title: M3GAN
films.note: Amy Donald's performance as the doll gets floated as a movement-as-acting nomination.

films.title: Are You There God? It's Me, Margaret.
films.note: Rachel McAdams named as a wish-list nominee.

segments.heading: Best Scenes, Red Carpets, and Netflix's Oscar Odds

films.title: Mission: Impossible – Dead Reckoning Part One
films.note: Its Rome chase named among Sean's grouping of best scenes.

films.title: John Wick: Chapter 4
films.note: The Sacré-Cœur sequence, same grouping.

films.title: Napoleon
films.note: The Austerlitz battle, same grouping; also the setting for Sean's story about a theater neighbor's running commentary.

films.title: The Killer
films.note: Its brute-force sequence named as a best-scene pick.

films.title: Anatomy of a Fall
films.note: The central fight scene named as a best-scene pick; also floated as a late addition to the Best Picture power rankings before losing out to The Color Purple.

films.title: Ferrari
films.note: Its argument scene, added as a late best-scene pick.

films.title: The Departed
films.note: Cited as the example of a Best Picture win where the audience actually wants to hear from the winner.

films.title: Green Book
films.note: Named as part of the Academy backlash that also hurt Roma's chances.

films.title: The Zone of Interest
films.note: Discussed as a contender jockeying for the last power-ranking spot.

films.title: Heart of Stone
films.note: Cited, dismissively, as the kind of Netflix movie Sean doesn't want to see more of.

films.title: Roma
films.note: Its Best Picture loss is called out as still painful, and as the moment Netflix's awards momentum stalled.

films.title: All Quiet on the Western Front
films.note: Named as an example of a Best Picture nominee nobody talks about a year later.

films.title: Top Gun: Maverick
films.note: Also the subject of an earlier tangent about its Best Original Song using an old officer-and-a-gentleman trick of folding the credits song into the score.

films.title: The Triplets of Belleville
films.note: One of Julia Roberts' ‘four faves’ on a recent red carpet.

films.title: Now, Voyager
films.note: Another of Julia Roberts' ‘four faves.’

films.title: The Adventures of Milo and Otis
films.note: The third of Julia Roberts' picks.

films.title: The Fall
films.note: Tarsem's film, Roberts' fourth pick, praised as a genuinely deep cut.

films.title: Nanook of the North
films.note: Mahershala Ali's red-carpet pick, cited as an early breakthrough in documentary filmmaking.

films.title: Oppenheimer
films.note: No. 1: still the favorite, though Sean thinks a Best Picture/Director split is looking more likely.

films.title: Killers of the Flower Moon
films.note: Getting a year-end critical bump.

films.title: Maestro
films.note: Not yet wide, but expected to climb once more people see it.

films.title: The Color Purple
films.note: No. 10: edges out Anatomy of a Fall in the final negotiation of the list.

films: Rebel Moon - Part One: A Child of Fire
